posts.name: Frank Ingriselli
posts.cooked:
For Berserker Day 2021, we’re offering a six-pack containing two bottles each of our first three vintages of Beau Rivage Chenin Blanc: 2017, 2018, and 2019. From organically farmed (but not certified) fruit grown in the alluvial soils of Clarksburg, California, it’s fermented in used cooperage and matured on the lees before bottling. You can find out more about the technicalities on our site. We’re actually just about to release the 2018 vintage, so the 2017 is coming from library stock, and the 2019 represents a preview of a wine that won’t be formally on the market for another year or so. But we were keen to offer three vintages together, because we have personally found it very instructive comparing them.

2017 was our first vintage, made back when William worked for Decanter Magazine and I had just started in the wine industry myself and was financed on a shoestring. It has taken a while to unwind, but today it might be our favorite of the three: it was quite a classic growing season and the wine is very nicely balanced at under 12 per cent alcohol (labelled at 12.1 to avoid making too much of a statement). In the last few months, a sort of oyster shell, gently reductive quality has emerged, something I caught glimpses of from barrel and which we really appreciate. This also has the advantage of being the most mature of these three vintages. Whereas the 2018 and 2019 were bottled under DIAM, which is neutral and very consistent, this was bottled under stupidly expensive certified TCA-free corks from Amorim, which consumed much of the modest profit margin (to my chagrin), and which I think contribute a touch of cork oak vanillin / lactones that didn’t come from the barrels.

2018 was a comparatively late, cool season, and the fruit sat on the vines without, disconcertingly, much happening. That’s reflected in the lowest alcohol level of the three vintages, 11.4 per cent if I remember correctly. There’s an interesting aromatic top-note that’s reminiscent of what William describes as Sauvignon “botanicals”, and which leads him to jokingly refer to this as, if people will forgive the lèse-majesté, our Vatan vintage. It’s taut and tangy, with the least texture for now but a lot of length, and I wouldn’t be surprised to see it turn out to be the best of the three in six or seven years. Right now, I like it best on day two; though admittedly that’s not a luxury that’s often afforded to a 27-dollar domestic Chenin.

2019 is the ripest wine of the three, landing at 12.4 per cent, and it was the most complicated of the three growing seasons by some margin. Yields were down by half, and you can feel that on the palate in the form of more volume and texture. The wine is still crisp and refreshing, but there’s more body and the salinity of the finish seems to be kicked up a notch. It’s the wine we know the least well as it wasn’t bottled that long ago so we’re still getting acquainted. We are especially pleased that the aromas remain quite classical in profile, testament to our efforts at sorting out botrytis. One of the things we hoped to demonstrate with this project is how one can take fruit that isn’t always exactly pampered in the winery and do interesting things to it with the right attention to detail and the right techniques, and the 2019 might be the vintage that best exemplifies that.

We had a lot of help and inspiration in starting this project, from the likes of Kalin Cellars’ Terry Leighton, Tegan Passalacqua, and Aron Weinkauf from Spottswoode. When we were trying to figure out haulage for our first harvest, the late Ulises Valdez offered to truck our fruit back to Saint Helena personally if we couldn’t find anyone to help—what a great guy he was! In Burgundy, people have gradually unwound and shared an idea or two with William, which has also been very instructive. So we hope that the wines do justice to all the advice and opportunities we’ve had, and we hope folks enjoy them!

The moment you all have been waiting for - Offer Details!


Today’s offer is a 6pk that contains two bottles of our 2017, 2018 and 2019 Chenin Blanc for $135 with $10 shipping to California and $20 shipping to the rest of the country. Finally, if you choose to purchase 12 bottles total, shipping is free! This is a saving of 20% on the bottles of wine and the shipping cost speaks for itself. I will be in the chat for any help that is needed or feel free to email us at info@beaurivagecellars.com with any questions. Follow the link below to purchase!

SOLD OUT

Thank you everyone for the support! If you were unable to get in on this specialty pack feel free to sign up for our mailing list for more information on future releases: https://beaurivagecellars.vinespring.site/#/register

Yeah, it’s a surprising place! The parcel we work with is right here: Google Maps

Amusingly, we a right next to a block of Valdigué which I believe goes into Korbel’s “Blanc de Noirs”.

There is a lot of untapped potential in Clarksburg, which has a climate that tracks pretty closely with the Yountville-Oakville sector of Napa Valley as it happens.

posts.name: Frank Ingriselli
posts.cooked:
Hi Everyone,

Sorry for my delay in posting here but I think I responded to a few of you over email. We are confident these wines will arrive safe on your doorstep. If there are any problems with your shipments when the wine arrives or if you have a flawed bottle down the road let me know and we will refund or replace no questions asked and no cost to you. Our goal is for you to enjoy our wines as we intend them and we are here to make sure that happens. Stay safe and feel free to email me at info@beaurivagecellars.com as I check that email more regularly than WB

posts.name: Ken Strauss
posts.cooked: Today I received notification that the wine was shipped. Arrival date is 2/16/2021.
High here on Sunday -2 low -17. Chenin Blanc slushy on Monday . Not ideal but Berserker wineriy’s customer service are the best, I’m not worried.

posts.name: Stephen Faulkner
posts.cooked: As has been noted in many threads, why? Why put all the effort into creating an artisanal product, the best wine you can, and then treat it like a toaster? Is the world ending on 2/20 so this is the only chance to ship? I buy wines to enjoy them, usually to celebrate life with friends and family. Dealing with flawed wine somewhere down the road just so I can break even financially is not a win for me. It is just a huge hassle to have to always be wondering, drink more bottles earlier than I want to check them, never really knowing if it is off, especially for a wine I have never had. So, it really diminishes the whole experience and is completely avoidable.

posts.name: Frank Ingriselli
posts.cooked: Sorry everyone I realize now I should have clarified that we had requested the wine not to ship to the cold states but unfortunately that was not done by our shipping company. I left that out yesterday evening so as not to seem that we were trying to shift the blame. Ultimately it is our responsibility if there are any issues and I hope my first post helps convey that. Thank you again for all your orders

posts.name: Michael O Brien
posts.cooked: It happens. Mine are scheduled for delivery Monday when the high is forecasted to be 14 degrees and the low 1 degree. That follows Sunday forecast high of 24 and low of 8. Might have Chenin Blanc popsicles.

posts.name: Robert Ferguson
posts.cooked:
I watched a TV segment years ago on challenging jobs. One was working in a rail yard when the temps are well below zero. The crew’s job was to constantly connect different strings of rail cars to a locomotive and move them around. The movement would keep the content in the tankers from freezing.

So as long as the train keeps moving, we’re good.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Waiting for mine, tomorrow, Tues 2/16. Supposed to be 8" of snow and 15 degrees.

I actually did an experiment last night: left three bottles outside in styrofoam and cardboard, and three bottles totally uncovered, overnight. Hit a low of 7F and was 11F at midnight, so pretty cold (for Texas, anyway). Obviously the uncovered bottles froze, but the bottles in the styrofoam didn’t and the wine temperature was 30F when I checked at 8am, in 4" of snow. So, horrible though it may be, styrofoam appears to be really quite effective! But let us know how your bottles fare!

posts.cooked:

My wine arrived. Packed in styrofoam (that had NOT disintegrated ). Was surprisingly NOT cold. Everything looks fine and in good shape - not frozen, no corks popped.

[Edit:] Already got an email from Frank. He must be tracking the shipments. His email was to ensure and check that everything arrived safely. Much appreciated.

posts.name: Anthony Curtas
posts.cooked:
Half my order arrived today as well (Virginia). Bottles were fridge cold, not frozen cold. Box and styro in great shape and no sign of bulged corks.

Gonna impatiently open one tonight to check on it and will report back.

Oh, and the winery contacted me as the box arrived to check on it, so they are tracking all this.

posts.name: Anthony Curtas
posts.cooked:
Okay, I’m really impatient. Just popped a 2017. It was very acidic and little fruit on open, but it was at around 45F. Let it warm up in the glass and the aromatics and fruit came out. Classic chenin profile. No sign of tartarate crystals and the cork was right where it should have been.

So I’m pretty confident my lot is fine but we are only flirting with freezing temps here. Best of luck to the rest of you with your orders.

posts.name: Stephen Faulkner
posts.cooked: Unfortunately, I was not as lucky as Steve. Just arrived, ice cold, with one cork popped, others apparently bulging to different degrees, others with no obvious outside signs. William, Frank - what is the protocol here? Replace all? Only ones that are obvious? Only ones that are obvious and suspect? Thank you.

posts.name: Abbie_Sal0v0n
posts.cooked: Mine just arrived, and there’s frost on the bottles. Not sure how this will affect the wine.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Unfortunately, I was not as lucky as Steve. Just arrived, ice cold, with one cork popped, others apparently bulging to different degrees, others with no obvious outside signs. William, Frank - what is the protocol here? Replace all? Only ones that are obvious? Only ones that are obvious and suspect? Thank you.

Personally, if it were wine I’d purchased, I’d keep everything that hasn’t pushed its cork at all, expecting it to be just fine. But, it’s your wine, that you purchased, and we want to make you happy, so it’s really up to you.

posts.name: Donna Murbach
posts.cooked:
I got mine today too, had 2 frozen bottles with completely popped corks and one slushy bottle with only a little bulge. And we’re in Georgia! It’s been cold here today, though, barely above freezing all day. Frank had emailed me as soon as he saw that the delivery was here, so I’ve responded and let him know already.

Edited to add: cold for us! Having lived in the upper Midwest and the northeast I realize that all y’all are going to be scoffing at my characterization of “cold,” which in some climes would be counted as a mild spring day.

posts.name: Jud Reis
posts.cooked: Ironic that mine arrived in Mass. and were just fine, then again we were warmer than Houston today!

posts.name: Dave McIsaac
posts.cooked: I got mine yesterday. Bottles were ice cold, but no pushed corks or tartrates.

posts.name: Ken Strauss
posts.cooked:
My bottles arrived safe and sound with no problems. The bottles were cool on touch.
Thanks

posts.name: Joshua Kates
posts.cooked:
Waiting for mine, tomorrow, Tues 2/16. Supposed to be 8" of snow and 15 degrees.

I actually did an experiment last night: left three bottles outside in styrofoam and cardboard, and three bottles totally uncovered, overnight. Hit a low of 7F and was 11F at midnight, so pretty cold (for Texas, anyway). Obviously the uncovered bottles froze, but the bottles in the styrofoam didn’t and the wine temperature was 30F when I checked at 8am, in 4" of snow. So, horrible though it may be, styrofoam appears to be really quite effective! But let us know how your bottles fare!

Hi,
I just received mine. They must have been sitting in warehouse or truck for a couple of days. In any case, three corks are totally out; the other three quite frosty but flat. It’s my busy day, I can send a picture later if you wish. I guess my preference would be to just get a new six, if they are available and on offer?

Thanks,
Josh
Received my shipment in Cleveland. 4 corks popped and ice floating in the other 2. If possible to get a new shipment I would appreciate it, but understand if all that is possible is a refund

posts.name: Dave McIsaac
posts.cooked: Sorry guys…that is a bummer

posts.name: Dan Kozarsky
posts.cooked: Mine were delivered today (in Mass.) and look fine. The wines were very cold but liquid, and no corks raised enough to disturb the capsule. Maybe crossing the middle of the country they were deep enough in the middle of the truck to avoid freezing?

posts.name: Brig Campbell
posts.cooked:
Received my shipment in Cleveland. 4 corks popped and ice floating in the other 2. If possible to get a new shipment I would appreciate it, but understand if all that is possible is a refund
beau rivage shipment.jpg

If the corks didn’t pop all the way out then it’ll be okay. freezing the wine won’t hurt it. Might precipitate some tartaric crystals if they didn’t cold stabilize the wine but those are flavorless anyways.

Don’t dump them, I guess is the story.

Are you saying if the corks are halfway out, the wine is ok?

I would respectfully disagree…

posts.name: Brian G r a f s t r o m
posts.cooked:
Dang … I’d be pretty unhappy with my shipping company right about now if I were Frank and William — sorry you guys got burned like this.

I only saw one person mention it, but it bears mentioning again: tartrate crystals. I avoid storing white wines (all wines, really, but I’d never consider it with a red) in my refrigerator for an extended period of time because it will cause tartaric acid to precipitate out of the solution (wine), which will have the effect of lowering the acidity in the wine. As an acid-freak, this is a situation I seek to avoid.
posts.display_username: Brian G r a f s t r o m

posts.cooked:

Considering the road conditions and the freezing weather in Plano, Tx for the past week, that makes total sense, but I am worried about the wines …

I too live in Plano. My wine arrived on Friday (19th). It was extremely cold but bottles were intact. I set it out at room temperature overnight and opened a bottle on Saturday evening. I typically would let wine rest a while before opening but I wanted to know the condition. The wine was is fine condition. Aromas and flavors were there. The remainder is resting in my wine cabinet. No harm done.
